slides.title: The Naked Object 😳
slides.content:
So far, our objects have been completely exposed.

Anyone writing code in the `main` method can grab `myDog.age` and set it to `-500`. That shouldn't be allowed!

This leads to messy code where external classes start messing with the internal variables of other objects.

slides.title: The Private Lock 🔒
slides.content: To prevent other developers (or yourself) from accidentally breaking an object's internal data, we use the `private` keyword.

quizData.question: If a variable is declared as `private`, who is allowed to read or change its value?

options.text: Methods written inside the very same Class where it was declared.

quizData.explanation: Private locks the variable inside its own Class. Methods inside that Class can see it, but no outside code can.

slides.title: The Public Open Door 🔓
slides.content:
## Public

The opposite of `private` is `public`. It means ANYONE in the entire program can see and modify the variable or method.

By default, if you don't write either word, Java makes it "package-private" (which is mostly public for beginners).

**Best Practice:** Always make your Attributes `private` by default to protect them!
